paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## GRAPH TREE NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In the field of deep learning, various architectures have been developed. However, most studies are limited to specific tasks or datasets due to their fixed layer structure. In this paper, we do not express the structure delivering information as a network model but as a data structure called a graph tree. And we propose two association models of graph tree neural networks(GTNNs) designed to solve the problems of existing networks by analyzing the structure of human neural networks. Defining the starting and ending points in a single graph is difficult, and a tree cannot express the relationship among sibling nodes. On the contrary, a graph tree(GT) can express leaf and root nodes as its starting and ending points and the relationship among sibling nodes. Instead of using fixed sequence layers, we create a GT for each data and train GTNN according to the tree’s structure. GTNNs are data-driven learning in which the number of convolutions varies according to the depth of the tree. Moreover, these models can simultaneously learn various types of datasets through the recursive learning method. Depth-first convolution (DFC) encodes the interaction result from leaf nodes to the root node in a bottom-up approach, and depth-first deconvolution (DFD) decodes the interaction result from the root node to the leaf nodes in a top-down approach. To demonstrate the performance of these networks, we conducted three experiments. In the first experiment, we verified whether it can be processed by combining graph tree neural networks and feature extraction networks(combining association models and feature extraction models). The second experiment is about whether the output of GTNN can embed information on all data contained in the GT(association). In the third experiment, we simultaneously learn the structure of images, sounds, graphs, and trees structure datasets (processing various datasets). We compared the performance of existing networks that separately learned image, sound, and natural language, relation datasets with the performance simultaneously learned by connecting these networks. As a result, these models learned without significant performance degradation, and the output vector contained all the information in the GT. And the importance of transfer-learning and fine-tuning in these models is explained.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In deep learning, various architectures have been designed such as CNN, RNN, and GNN(LeCun et al., 1989; Hopfield, 1982; Scarselli et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2020). These networks show good performance in various fields such as image, text, and sound etc. However, most of the existing studies are focused on a specific dataset or task. And there are many tasks that humans can do but are difficult to perform with neural networks.

First, some of the difficulties with imitating human neural networks are introduced below. (C-i) Sensory organs that process information for each data type exist at several starting points. (C-ii) A tree cannot express the relationship between sibling nodes, and it is difficult to define the graph’s starting point and ending point. (C-iii) The structure that receives information is hierarchical and freely integrated with various structures. But existing networks are designed to learn only specific tasks or datasets because layers are fixed. (C-iv) The number of activated neurons and the processing depths differ depending on the data type and complexity. And sometimes the information is entered and sometimes not. (C-v) Human neurons are very numerous and process various types of data.

C-[ _·_ ] denotes the characteristics. (C-i) Humans have various sensory organs from which they receive information such as sight, hearing, and smell, and transfer it to the cerebral cortex using different

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

information-processing organs. The optic nerve also begins with the visual cortex of the occipital

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Human brain (b) Graph Tree
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Comparison of the human brain and graph tree neural network

lobe, and the auditory nerve begins with the auditory cortex of the temporal lobe; this means that the two paths have different starting points. (C-ii) A graph can describe the relationship depending on whether all nodes are connected, but it is not easy to define the start and end points. A tree is a data structure that can define direction with the leaf and root nodes. On the other hand, a tree structure cannot express the relationship between sibling nodes. (C-iii) Information from different sensory organs is gathered in the association area. Typically, the posterior parietal cortex(Malach et al., 1995) is located at the top of the head as part of the parietal lobe, and sensory information such as vision and hearing is fused and interpreted; Then, information is coordinated in the frontal lobe and humans act. (C-iv) There is also an over-fitting problem in DNN(Widrow et al., 1960), and various attempts have been made to solve this in (Srivastava et al., 2014). In DNN, simply deeper layers lead to an over-fitting problem(Dai et al., 2017). In particular, NasNet(Zoph et al., 2018) has attempted to design a layer automatically through reinforcement learning and RNN. This means that there is an appropriate network depth depending on the complexity of the dataset; we want to design a network that could adjust the depth of the layers according to the complexity of each data, not the data set. (C-v) Also, the number of human neurons is about 85 billion(Herculano-Houzel, 2009), which is difficult to express in a network. Therefore, GTNNs are designed to solve these problems.

A-[ _·_ ] denotes our approach of C-[ _·_ ]. (A-i) This network can perform information processing, such as that done by sensory organs before their signals are entered into the cerebral cortex. In this paper, we express this sensory organ as a feature-extraction process(sec.2.2). Type information is stored with the data and converted into an input vector by different feature extraction processes for each type. (A-ii) We propose a new data structure called Graph Tree Node(GTN) and Graph Tree(GT). A graph tree can be expressed using leaf and root nodes as the starting and ending points, as well as the relationship among sibling nodes. In addition, we can modify the existing tree dataset at no high cost. (A-iii) subtrees of different types are merged and make a final decision at the root node; this structure is difficult to express with the existing forward-learning method. Therefore, we used a recursive-learning method(Goller & Kuchler, 1996). This methodology has been effective in analyzing the semantics of programming source code or natural language(Socher et al., 2013; Mou et al., 2014). We call the recursive-convolution methodology used in GTNNs the depth-first convolution(DFC) methodology(sec.2.3). DFC is a convolution method in which subtrees originating from different Leaf nodes are integrated into a bottom-up approach and represent hierarchical and relational information. GTNNs can simultaneously learn various types of datasets through the recursive learning method because the graph tree data structure can express various model structures. (A-iv) GTNNs are data-driven learning in which the number of convolutions varies according to the depth of the tree. Instead of using fixed sequence layers, we create a graph tree for each data and learn according to the tree’s structure. If we modify this network further, we can adjust the depth according to the type and complexity of each data. In addition, We introduce two models of GTNNs called graph tree convolutional networks(GTC) and graph tree recursive networks(GTR), and these models are mathematically related to MLP(Hinton et al., 2006) and RNN. The Fully Connected layer(FC layer) in MLP as Level Layer of GTC, and Time in RNN as depth of GTR on a special case. (A-v) The number of human neurons is enormous, and it is difficult to express all of their structures in a network; on the other hand, this network can represent multiple neurons because it can perform recursive end-to-end learning according to the amount of information.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

This network theory holds that "units of information have a relationship in the form of a graph, then become a bigger unit of information, and have a relationship with other units of information. At this point, the unit of information is a set of neurons, and we can express it as a vector with GTNN."

To demonstrate the performance of this network, we conducted three experiments. And we used several benchmark datasets (image(MNIST(LeCun et al., 1998)), sound(Speech Commands(Warden, 2018)), text(IMDB(Maas et al., 2011),SST(Socher et al., 2013)), graph(Dou et al., 2021)).

In the first experiment, It verifies whether feature extraction networks and association networks can be learned together. we compared the performance of existing networks that separately learned image(LeNet-5(LeCun et al., 1989), sound(M5(Dai et al., 2017)), text(CNN(Kim, 2014)) with the performance simultaneously learned by connecting these networks for feature extraction into GTNN.

The second experiment contained one or three types of data into a GT, and GTNNs learned these GTs. Then, we verified whether the output contained all the information inside the GT. Then, we conducted a verification experiment on whether the network’s output contained all information on the tree.

In the third experiment, we verifies whether data of various structures(image, sound, tree, graph) can be learned and checked the plot of the learning process in appendix E. As a result, the network was learned without significant degradation in performance compared to when learning existing networks separately, and all information on the tree could be embedded as a vector.

## 2 GRAPH TREE NEURAL NETWORKS

In this section, we proposes graph tree architecture. The architecture consists of three parts
(i) Defining the data structure of graph tree node(GTN) and designing the graph tree structure (GT).

(ii) Defining the feature-extraction(FE) process. (iii) Defining the GTNN model.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Graph Tree Node (b) Feature Extraction Networks (c) Graph Tree Neural Network
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 2: Graph Tree Architecture

## 2.1 GRAPH TREE DATA STRUCTURE

The graph tree (GT) is a data structure that can express relational and hierarchical information.

- _x_ : (Input) – we can store the data such as images, sound, text, or tabular to each node.

- _τ_ : (Type) – This refers to the type of information matching the input _x_ . the feature-extraction function( _ψ_ ) is selected by _τ_ like Eqn.1.
- **A** _c_ : (Children Adjacency Matrix) – This refers to the relationship information that exists in GTN. the number of children is _N_ and we can express it as **A** _c ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ .

- **C** : (Children) – This refers to the child nodes of GTN matching to the node of **A** _c_ . If there is no matching child, it is replaced with **GTN** _∅_ , which carries the initial hidden state _[−→]_ 0 instead. We can express this as **C** = _{_ **GTN** 1 _,_ **GTN** 2 _, .._ **GTN** _N }_ .

We can express **GTN** = _{x, τ,_ **A** _c,_ **C** _}_ , **GTN** _i ∈_ **GT** . **GTN** _root_ denotes the root node of **GT** . The reason for defining the relationship among child nodes is the convenience of implementation. If we define GTN in the above way, we can convert the tree dataset into the GT dataset without significantly modifying the tree structure that has previously been useful.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 2.2 FEATURE EXTRACTION NETWORKS & TYPE BIAS

_−→ x_ = Ψ( _x, τ_ ) = [ _ψτ_ ( _x_ ) _, onehot_ ( _τ_ )] (1)

The _x_ (input) and _τ_ (type) exist together in GTN, and the feature extraction process of _x_ is selected by _τ_ as Eqn 1. We expressed the function of feature extraction as Ψ, and this function converts _x_ to _[−→] x_ . And the one-hot vector has the effect of having a different bias value for each type( _τ_ ). The weight parameter corresponding to the type one-hot vector means the bias value for the corresponding type, and the activation threshold value is adjusted for each type-bias. It can represent data type information and that it is transmitted from which nerve. In addition, we do not need to perform adding operations(+) for bias. Therefore, the empty space of GT can be used as a zero-vector when batch learning is performed. And the dictionary structure is very useful for batch learning; we attached the methodology to appendix.A. This methodology divides mini-batch samples by type, and the type-mini-batch size varies each time. Therefore, we recommend using a weight standardization(Qiao et al., 2019) or group normalization(Wu & He, 2018) to avoid batch normalization(Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) affected by the batch size. We described the details of the network we used in appendix.C.

## 2.3 DEPTH-FIRST CONVOLUTION & DECONVOLUTION

Figure 3: Depth First Convolution & Deconvolution

This section introduces two propagation methods of depth-first convolution (DFC) (a convolution method for traversing all nodes of GT) and depth-first deconvolution (DFD). Depth-first search (DFS) is a search algorithm for the tree data structure. Unlike general recursive convolution, DFC and DFD are methodologies for learning children’s relationships, feature extraction networks together.

**Algorithm 1** Depth First Convolution

**Post-order Depth-first convolution (Left-First)** If DFS is post-order, iteration starts from the leaf node. Likewise, DFC is a recursive convolution that propagates from the deepest nodes. GTNN Utilizes the hidden vector expressing the current depth and state.

First, we need to bring four items( _xp, τp, Acp, Cp_ ) of the current **GTN** _p_ . the p means the convolution order, and _xp_ is embedded by the feature extraction network(Ψ) according to the type( _τp_ ) and becomes the feature vector( _[−→] x p_ ).

Graph tree network models perform the convolution by receiving information( _[−→] x[′] pq,[−→] h[′] pq_ ) from the current node’s children and the relationship( _Acp_ ) among children’s information. We can use this process like learning the relationship between siblings nodes. the _[−→] h p_ means the convolution output of the child nodes. finally, _[−→] x p_ and _[−→] h p_ are delivered to the parent node. If the child node does not exist( **C** _p_ ), the current node means a leaf node. Therefore, the feature vector( _[−→] x p_ ) and initial hidden state( _[−→]_ 0 ) are transmitted to the parent node.

The important thing is that the root node only receives information from their children and does not the convolution with the root feature vector. Therefore, like algo.3, convolution is performed once more on the output of the DFC. If we repeat this process, we finally get _[−→] h root_ . Additionally, we can store information( _∗i_ 1 _, ∗i_ 2) generated during the DFC process in **GTN** _.more_ and use it during the DFD process (ex. If the aggregate function is a maxpool, we can use the indices information during deconvolution).

**Pre-order Depth-first Deconvolution (Right-First)** DFD is a decoding methodology for GTNNs. The DFC propagates from the leaf node to the root node, whereas the DFD propagates from the root node to the leaf node in the order of pre-order depth first to decode this.

First, we need to bring three items( _τp, Acp, Cp_ ) of the current **GTN** _p_ . _x_ ˜ _p_ is decoded by the feature decoder network(Ψ _[−]_[1] ) according to the type( _τp_ ) and the decoded output is stored in the **GTN** _p.x_ ˆ _p_ .

If the child does not exist, this means a leaf node and performs a return. Otherwise, The hidden state( _h_[˜] _p_ ) is restored to **˜x** _[′] p[,]_ **[ ˜h]** _[′] p_[through] **[ GTNN]** _[−]_[1][; and] **[ ˜h]** _[′] p_[is transferred to child nodes. Also, if DFC] proceeds from left to right as left first (1 _...N_ ), DFD proceeds from right to left as right first ( _N..._ 1). Please see Fig.3.

Like the propagate function (algo.3), we can freely encode and decode through DFC and DFD. We want to show the relationship between DFC and DFD and the possibility of expanding to the widely used autoencoder(Hinton & Salakhutdinov, 2006) models. In addition, Implementing this recursive function as a loop(while) will speed up.

## 2.4 ASSOCIATION NEURAL NETWORK MODELS : GTR & GTC SERIES

This section proposes GTR and GTC series that perform inductive learning in GTNNs. We describe the convolution performed in one graph tree node by DFC. These networks can be used as an association cell or layer, and we explained the difference between the two models in appendix B.

These association models do not use any fixed architecture and integrate information according to the GT structure. And by fusing graph and tree structures, we can learn more diverse data structures with one cell and various architectures can be expressed by data(please see the appendix.D). Accordingly, these models are freer to perform various tasks and learn a lot of information using fewer parameters.

These association models are as follows: The feature extraction models create the information from data; It is the "smallest unit" of information in the GT. The parent GTN receives information from their children and perform the convolution operation with the relation term; the convolution output is a "bigger unit" of information. Finally, a root GTN has all GT information and perform the convolution operation; the output is "the biggest unit" of information.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 2.4.1 ASSOCIATION CELL : GRAPH TREE RECURSIVE NEURAL NETWORK

We will describe a GTR cell that trains the GT described previously in Sec 2.1. GTR model can be expressed as **GTR** = _{_ **W** _,_ Ψ _, g, σ}_ **W** _∈_ R _[F][ ′][×]_[(] _[F]_[ +] _[B]_[+] _[F][ ′]_[)] . _F_ is the node-feature size, _B_ is the type-bias size, and _F[′]_ is the output and hidden size, which is the information received from the child node. Ψ denotes the feature-extraction(Eqn 1) process described above, _g_ denotes the aggregate function, and _σ_ denotes the activation function.

The propagation for GTNN is proceeding by the DFC traversing from leaf nodes to a root node (sec.2.3). _p_ is the order of DFC, _q_ is the child node number, _Np_ is the number of children of **GTN** _p_ , _xp_ is the node-input of **GTN** _p_ .

The Ψ extracts the feature vector( _[−→] x p_ ) from _xp_ of **GTN** _p_ through the feature extraction process(Eqn.2) considering the type information( _τp_ ). The first location to start is leaf nodes by DFC. In the Leaf node, the initial hidden state is a zero vector( _[−→]_ 0 ), and this is concatenated with _[−→] x p_ to become [ _[−→] x p,[−→] h p_ ] _∈_ R _[F]_[ +] _[B]_[+] _[F][ ′]_ through the concatenation process as [ _,_ ] and then delivered to the parent node. If a child node exists, the current node receives information from a child node as [ _x[−→][′] pq[,][ −→] h[′] pq_[]][.] _∥[N] q_ =0 _[p]_[[] _[−→] x[′] pq,[−→] h[′] pq_ ] _[T]_ means that [ _[−→] x[′] pq,[−→] h[′] pq_ ] matching the graph nodes( **A** _cp_ ) existing in the p-th node are stacked as much as _Np_ , which is _∥[N] q_ =0 _[p]_[[] _[−→] x[′] pq,[−→] h[′] pq_ ] _[T] ∈_ R _[N][p][×]_[(] _[F]_[ +] _[B]_[+] _[F][ ′]_[)] .

We applied the GCN methodology(Kipf & Welling, 2016), which is useful in the GNN field. Therefore we expressed as **A**[˜] _cp_ = **A** _cp_ + **I** , where **I** is the Identity matrix. If we express the connection value as _−_[1] _−_[1] 1, just the more connected nodes becames the scale value larger than others. Therefore, **D**[˜] _cp_ 2 **[A]**[˜] _cp_ **[D]**[˜] _cp_ 2 is applied using the order matrix **D**[˜] _cp_ of **A**[˜] _cp_ as a method of normalizing the relationship matrix in Eqn. 3. we used _F_ as 128, _B_ as 3(image, sound, language), and _F[′]_ as 128, and _σ_ as ReLU(Nair & Hinton, 2010), and g was the readout of Max. If there is no sibling node of the current node, GTR is mathematically identical to the RNN as **IW** [ _[−→] x ,[−→] h_ ]. RNN can be a special case of GTR, and the depth of the tree means times of the RNN.

## 2.4.2 ASSOCIATION LAYERS : GRAPH TREE CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK

GTC is composed of **GTC** = _{{_ **W** 0 _, ..._ **W** _m},_ Ψ _, g, σ}_ . There is **W** _lv ∈_ R _[F] lv[ ′][×]_[(] _[F][lv]_[+] _[B]_[+] _[F] lv[ ′]_ +1[)] in charge of each level, and the network in charge of each level is called the level layer. The input size of the level is _Flv_ , its output size is _Flv[′]_[, and the output size of the child is] _[ F][ ′] lv_ +1[. Therefore, it is possible] to adjust the input, hidden size; and it can learn the depth-limited tree, _m_ means Maximum-Depth. We set _Flv_ and _Flv[′]_[to 128 for all lvs in the same way as GTR in the experiment. If the input size of] _Flv_ is 0 and there is no sibling node of the current node, this network is mathematically identical to the FC layer as **IW** _lv[−→] x[T]_ and the MLP as ( **IW** 0 _.._ ( **IW** _lv−_ 1( **IW** _lv[−→] x[T]_ ))), **I** is the Identity matrix. The mathematical expression of GTC is similar to Eqn 3. The depth of the tree indicates the number of layers of the MLP. Therefore, the MLP and FC layer can be a special case of GTC.

## 2.5 ATTENTION MODELS

There is an attention process in the human brain. Therefore, GATs(Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017), a model that has been useful recently, was combined.

## 2.5.1 GRAPH TREE RECURSIVE ATTENTION NETWORKS

We introduce GTRAs that learn the importance through attention by slightly modifying the expression of the GTR. It is composed of **GTRAs** = _{_ **W** _,_ Ψ _, g, σ, σa,[−→] a }_ that added _{σa,[−→] a }_ in GTR. A parameter for attention mechanism is added (R[2] _[F][ ′] ×_ R[2] _[F][ ′] →_ R) and the attention’s activation function used LeakyReLU(Xu et al., 2015) in the same way as GATs. _Npq_ is a set of nodes connected

to the q-th child’s node in the _Acp_ of **GTN** _p_ , and we can express this as:

With the attention methodology introduced in GATs(Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017), it learns how the r-th node is of importance to the q-th node. This information is replaced with the part to which the adjacency matrix is connected.

If **A** _cp_ does not exist in the GTN, we utilized to select critical features as :

The self-node is set to 1 and the relationship values of the node are replaced with a real value of 0 _∼_ 1 using Sigmoid. Therefore, we can train the attention learning without **A** _cp_ . It means to learn relationships based on self-node. Other useful models is self-adaptive model(Wu et al., 2019).

Therefore, the GTRAs are as follows:

Therefore it is][ [] **[x]** _[′][p][,]_ **[ h]** _[′][p]_[]] _[ ∈]_[R] _[N][p][×]_[(] _[F]_[ +] _[B]_[+] _[F][ ′]_[)][.] To further stabilize the self-attention process, we introduce a multi-head attention mechanism:

We set K to 8 and set output dim for each head to 16(= _Flv[′][/K]_[). therefore we set] _[ F][ ′] lv_ to 128. This process becomes a cell and delivers the result from the leaf node to the root node.

2.5.2 GRAPH TREE ATTENTION NETWORKS

The GTAs model is modified from GTC, and GATs’ attention mechanism was applied. this model can be expressed as **GTAs** = _{{_ **W** 0 _, ..._ **W** _m}, {[−→] a_ 0 _, ...[−→] a m},_ Ψ _, g, σ, σa}_ , and _{σa,[−→] a lv}_ are added in _′_ GTC( _a[−→] lv ∈_ R[2] _[F] lv_ ). Unlike GTRAs, GTAs can design different sizes of _F, F[′]_ to match the lv. this model is designed to select critical features, and it is similar to Sec.2.5.1

**Supervised learning** Supervised learning uses datasets with labels. In this network, the _[−→] h root_ was mapped to the dimension of the class using a fully connected layer( _F[′]_ to class count) and we calculated the log softmax and negative log likelihood loss for supervised learning as: ˆ _y_ = _log_  softmax_ ( _TaskLayer_ ( _[−→] h root_ )) (9) _loss_ = _negative_  log_  likelihood_  loss_ ( _y,_ ˆ _y_ ) (10)

## 3 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

This section introduces the contents of the experiment and the GT dataset consisting of the image, sound, and natural language. How to design GT is introduced in appendix.D.

**Datasets** Our goal is to express the information delivery process of existing models as a graph tree of data. The first experiment is whether various datasets can be simultaneously learned by combining GTNN and feature extraction networks. the GT we used in the experiment is illustrated by Fig.4(a). Layer node means that it has no input value, only performs convolution. If there is no sibling node, GTC performs the same operation as one FC layer. Sound and natural language GT have two nodes, and image GT has three nodes because It was configured similarly to figure (likes this fig.1). The

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Expt 1,2: One type (b) Expt 2: All to sound (c) Expt 2: All to image (d) Expt 2: All to language
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 4: Graph tree datasets of the experiments

important point is not limited to any specific architecture; later, this GT could be modified according to the task or complexity.

The second experiment is about whether the output( _[−→] h root_ ) of GTNN is embedded information on all data contained in the GT(association). Therefore, we performed image, sound, and text classification by putting image, sound, text data in one GT, and we also learned the GT used in the first experiment simultaneously. One problem is that when the three types of data are combined, The number of combinations of the dataset becomes too huge. For example, there are 50,000 images, 105,000 sounds, and 17,500 natural language samples, and when these data are combined, we need to generate 50,000 _×_ 105,000 _×_ 17,500 GT samples. For this problem, we used a sampling method. When loading each data, two data of different types are sampled randomly. Thus, when we load one data, two GTs are generated of the one type GT and the three type GT. The test accuracy is the result of averaging 5-fold. the GT dataset of the second experiment is illustrated by Fig.4(a,b,c,d). In the third experiment, we added the graph and tree structure dataset, This is described in Appendix E.

## 3.1 THE EXPERIMENT 1 : INDIVIDUAL & SIMULTANEOUS (PROCESSING VARIOUS DATASETS)

Table 1: the result of Experiment 1

We trained without using a pre-trained model to check whether various feature extraction models can be learned simultaneously. Only word embedding network(Pennington et al., 2014) was used as a pretrained model. Existing networks learn only about specific datasets. Therefore, LeNet-5(LeCun et al., 1998), M5(Dai et al., 2017), and CNN(Kim, 2014) were selected for feature extraction networks of the image(MNIST(LeCun et al., 1998)), sound(Speech Command(Warden, 2018)), and natural language(IMDB(Maas et al., 2011)). We described more details in appendix C. The reason for choosing these networks is based on Convolutional Neural Networks specialized extract features.

Since the number of classes is different for each data, the test was divided into two cases. (Task Layers): The last layer was placed differently for each dataset and mapped to the class dimension of the dataset. (47 class): The one last layer was used for all datasets, and the number of class dimensions is equal to the sum of class dimensions of all datasets. Because if the last layer is placed differently for each dataset, information on the input type may be informed. All models were learned during 30 epochs, and the results were as Table.1(Task layers, 47 class), and the learning process is (Fig.5(a)).

The learning processes of GTNNs are similar to the process of individually learning feature extraction networks; if the model learns like this, several problems are found. LeNet-5 is well trained, but M5

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Simultaneous-learning (47class) (b) Transfer-learning (c) Fine-Tuning
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 5: Test Accuracy (The Experiment 1)

needs to be more trained, and CNN has an overfitting problem. The reason is that each network has different epochs to have optimal performance, and setting learning parameters is more complex than learning individually.

**transfer learning & fine-tuning** Therefore, after learning individually, we combined the association model to perform transfer-learning and fine-tuning(Pan & Yang, 2009). The result is Table.1(Transferlearning, Fine-tuning). In transfer learning, the parameters of feature extraction networks are not modified, and in fine-tuning, the parameters are modified. We used the validation set to learn each feature extraction network and adopt the lowest value of the validation loss to use the model. As a result, the LeNet-5, M5, CNN were pre-trained with 26, 95, 3 epochs. These models were combined with GTNN to perform fine-tuning and transfer learning. Then, the network was re-learned, and learning was stopped at epochs with the lowest value of validation loss using each of the same validation datasets. As in the results of Table.1(Transfer-learning, Fine-tuning) and Fig.5(b,c), in the case of transfer learning, performance was poor in the IMDB dataset. We thought it was overfitting because the IMDB dataset was relatively small. We reduced the learning rate from 0.001 to 0.0001, but the results were similar. While fine-tuning was similar to the performance learned individually in all datasets. And the performance improved compared to when fine-tuning was not performed. Consequently, If transfer learning and fine tuning are used by setting parameters well according to the network, the performance of the network can be improved. In Experiment 3, we learn datasets of various structures(Image, sound, tree, graph) through GTNN. It is described in Appendix.E

3.2 THE EXPERIMENT 2 : CAN ALL DATA INFORMATION BE CONTAINED? (ASSOCIATION)

Table 2: the result of association test

We constructed the GT dataset described above(Fig.4(a,b,c,d)) to validate if the output vector can contain all the information in the GT, and the results are as follows Table.2. The meaning of FE Baseline is the performance of networks of LeNet-5, M5, and CNN. In these experiments, it has been verified that it is possible to learn various types of datasets using one network cell and that information can be embedded together. Consequently, we can share an association cell or layers to learn without being limited to the dataset type and embed all information inside the GT into a vector.

## 4 CONCLUSION

We introduced a data-driven network that can jointly learn relationships and hierarchical information. This study is to be free from architecture and has been developed to connect various types of information being developed. And The author of this paper believes that one day a neural network will emerge that can perform all human tasks. In addition, this is an association model that behaves like human sensory organs and can be described as similar to a human neural network. We think this network can be used as a deep neural network part of DQN(Mnih et al., 2013). We will try to leverage this network to approach problems that have not been solved before. This paper is part of a series; in the next paper, we introduce Graph Tree Deductive Networks(GTDs).

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents an ambitious and conceptually interesting framework—Graph Tree Neural Networks (GTNNs)—that aims to unify various data modalities through a recursive, data-driven architecture inspired by human neural processing. The core idea of using graph trees as dynamic computational graphs is novel and has potential for handling heterogeneous, structured data. The authors provide reasonable motivation, propose two model variants (GTC and GTR), and demonstrate experimental results across multiple datasets. However, several technical and logical issues undermine confidence in the full realizability and correctness of the proposed methods. Key concerns include inconsistent mathematical formulations, ambiguous algorithm definitions, and claims about equivalence to MLP/RNN that are not rigorously supported. While the vision is compelling, the technical execution lacks precision in critical areas.

comments.title: Inconsistent hidden state concatenation in GTR description
comments.quote: In the Leaf node, the initial hidden state is a zero vector( [−→] 0 ), and this is concatenated with [−→] x p to become [ [−→] x p,[−→] h p ] ∈ R [F] + [B] + [F] ′ through the concatenation process as [ _,_ ] and then delivered to the parent node.
comments.explanation: The text states that the feature vector [−→]x_p and the zero hidden state [−→]0 are concatenated into a vector of dimension F + B + F′. However, [−→]x_p already includes both the extracted features and the type-bias (one-hot), as defined in Equation 1: Ψ(x, τ) = [ψ_τ(x), onehot(τ)]. Therefore, [−→]x_p is already in R^{F+B}, so the concatenated vector should be [[−→]x_p, [−→]h_p] ∈ R^{F+B+F′}. This matches the dimensionality claim. But the issue arises when this structure is used in GTR: the input to the weight matrix W ∈ R^{F′×(F+B+F′)} assumes this full dimension. The derivation seems consistent with the final dimensions, so the confusion is more in exposition than error. However, the phrasing implies that the zero vector is added *before* concatenation with [−→]x_p, which could mislead a reader into thinking three-way concatenation occurs. Given that Equation 1 already defines [−→]x_p to include both feature and bias, and Algorithm 1 uses Ψ to produce [−→]x_p directly, the rest of the system appears consistent. This is better classified as ambiguous phrasing rather than a true inconsistency.

comments.title: Misleading equivalence claim between GTR and RNN
comments.quote: If there is no sibling node of the current node, GTR is mathematically identical to the RNN as **IW** [ [−→] x ,[−→] h ]. RNN can be a special case of GTR, and the depth of the tree means times of the RNN.
comments.explanation: The claim that GTR reduces to an RNN when there are no siblings is questionable. While both are recursive, an RNN processes a sequence with shared parameters across time steps, updating a hidden state as h_t = σ(W [x_t, h_{t-1}]). In contrast, GTR as described uses depth-first convolution over a tree, where each node may have different children and the processing order is structural, not sequential. Even with a single child per node (unary tree), GTR applies the same recursive update, but it does so across a tree traversal, not a time-indexed sequence. Moreover, RNNs typically assume a fixed temporal order, while DFC is a structural traversal. The update rule may resemble an RNN cell, but the architectural semantics—especially the use of aggregate functions over child nodes and graph-based relation modeling—diverge significantly. The authors are correct that the recurrence pattern is similar, but stating that GTR is *mathematically identical* to RNN overstates the equivalence. A more accurate claim would be that the node update in a unary tree resembles an RNN cell update, but the overall model semantics differ due to structural recursion and the absence of a global time index.

comments.title: Incorrect matrix dimension in GTC definition
comments.quote: There is **W** _lv ∈_ R _[F] lv[ ′][×]_[(] _[F][lv]_[+] _[B]_[+] _[F] lv[ ′]_ +1[)] in charge of each level
comments.explanation: The dimensionality of W_lv is given as R^{F'_lv × (F_lv + B + F'_{lv+1})}. However, the input to level lv should consist of the current node's feature (size F_lv), type-bias (B), and the *child's hidden output*, which is in R^{F'_{child}}. In a level-wise setting, if the next level corresponds to children, then F'_{lv+1} is the child's hidden size. But the notation suggests F'_{lv+1} is the output size of the child level, which would be correct only if the children are processed at depth lv+1. The problem is that the output of the child is in R^{F'_{lv+1}}, so the concatenation [x_p, bias, h'_child] makes sense. But the subscript on W_lv suggests it maps from (F_lv + B + F'_{lv+1}) to F'_lv. That would mean increasing depth corresponds to increasing lv, but the input includes F'_{lv+1}, which is *from a deeper level*. This creates a circular dependency: W_lv depends on F'_{lv+1}, which should be defined later. In reality, in a bottom-up pass, the child's hidden state h' is computed first (at deeper depth), then the parent uses it. So lv should index depth from root down, but F'_{lv+1} would then be *smaller* depth. This is a notational confusion. A more standard approach would be to define W_d for depth d, taking input from children at depth d+1. The current indexing is ambiguous and potentially incorrect if lv increases toward the root. The dimension should likely be R^{F'_d × (F_d + B + F'_{d+1})} with d increasing toward leaves. As written, it could mislead a reader about the flow of information.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses undefined variable and has off-by-one error in loop
comments.explanation: The loop line is heavily corrupted by OCR artifacts. It appears to mean: 'for q ← 1 to Np do', but the text shows 'q−→x[′] ←pq, 1 [−→] h...N[′] pq p←'. The variables [−→]x[′]_pq and [−→]h[′]_pq are assigned inside the loop via DFC, so they cannot appear in the loop header. This is clearly an OCR or formatting error. The intended meaning, from context and line 7, is that q iterates over child indices from 1 to Np (number of children). However, in most programming and pseudocode conventions, array indices start at 1 or 0. Line 7 appends [x'[pq], h'[pq]], suggesting C_p[q] is valid for q from 1 to Np. But in line 10, the condition is 'len(C_p) is 0', and C_p is defined as {GTN_1, ..., GTN_N}, so indexing starts at 1. The loop should be 'for q = 1 to Np'. The OCR corruption makes the code unreadable, but given the surrounding context and standard DFS conventions, the intent is clear. However, for reproducibility, the loop bounds must be precise. Assuming 1-based indexing, the loop should be 'for q = 1 to Np'. The current form is underspecified and could lead to off-by-one errors in implementation.

comments.title: Missing definition of GTNN Stack and inverse in algorithms
comments.explanation: The function 'GTNN Stack' is used in Algorithm 1 (line 15) and 'GTNN^−1' in Algorithm 2 (line 8), but these are never defined in the paper. From context, GTNN Stack appears to be a neural module that takes the children's features and hidden states, the adjacency matrix A_c, and possibly the current level, and produces a new hidden state and possibly updated features. But the exact structure—whether it's a GCN layer, a multi-layer perceptron, or something else—is not specified. The paper mentions using GCN methodology (Kipf & Welling, 2016), but does not define the actual equation for the aggregation. This is a critical gap: without knowing how children's representations are aggregated, the model cannot be reproduced. The same applies to GTNN^{-1}, which is used in deconvolution. The lack of a formal equation for the core transformation undermines the methodological clarity. While Section 2.4.1 introduces GTR with parameters W and functions, it does not directly connect to 'GTNN Stack' in the algorithm. A mapping between the high-level model and the algorithmic function is missing.

comments.title: Ambiguity in DFD algorithm regarding node type access
comments.quote: 1: **function** DFD( _x_ ˜ _p, h_[˜] _p,_ **GTN** _p, ∗lv_ ) 2: _τp,_ **A** _cp,_ **C** _p ←_ **GTN** _p.dconv_  items_ ()
comments.explanation: The use of **GTN_p.dconv_items()** is introduced without definition. In Algorithm 1, the items are retrieved via **GTN_p.items()**, which returns (x, τ, A_c, C). In DFD, the function dconv_items() is used and omits x, which makes sense because x is provided as input (˜x_p). However, τ_p is still needed for the feature decoder Ψ^{-1}. The text says: '˜x_p is decoded by the feature decoder network(Ψ^{−1}) according to the type(τ_p)', so τ_p must be accessible. But **GTN_p** is passed as a node that already has embedded information (e.g., from DFC), so its original input x might have been replaced or augmented. The omission of x from dconv_items is reasonable. However, the function dconv_items() is never defined, nor is it explained what 'GTN_p' contains post-DFC. This is a notable underspecification: during DFD, the model needs τ_p to route ˜x_p to the correct decoder, but it's unclear whether τ_p is stored separately or overwritten. Given that type is metadata, it should persist. The intent is plausible, but the implementation details are missing, making reproduction difficult.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting concept of Graph Tree Neural Networks (GTNNs) that aim to create data-driven architectures capable of handling multiple data types simultaneously. The motivation is well-grounded in biological inspiration and practical limitations of fixed-architecture networks. However, the paper suffers from several technical issues including notation inconsistencies, incomplete algorithm descriptions, and missing mathematical details that hinder reproducibility and clarity. The experimental results show promise but the methodology description has gaps that make it difficult to fully evaluate the claims.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in DFC algorithm
comments.explanation: The notation here is inconsistent with Equation (1) which defines the feature extraction output as _[−→] x_ = Ψ(x, τ). The algorithm should use _[−→] x p_ instead of _x p_ to maintain consistency. This appears to be a typographical error but could cause confusion for readers trying to implement the algorithm.

comments.title: Incomplete notation in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: This line appears to have incomplete or incorrect notation. The trailing arrow _−→_ without any attached variable doesn't match the notation used elsewhere in the paper. Based on the context and line 16 which stores both (_∗i_ 1, _∗i_ 2), this likely should be storing only _∗i_ 1, but the notation needs correction.

comments.title: Typo in DFD algorithm
comments.quote: 5: _τp,_ **A** _cp,_ **C** _p ←_ **GTN** _p.dconv_ items_ ()
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo in this line with '.dconv items ()' which doesn't match standard Python syntax. Based on the context and similar line 2 in Algorithm 1, this should likely be '**GTN** _p.items ()' to extract the items from the GTN object.

comments.title: Missing key mathematical equations
comments.quote: we expressed as **A**[˜] _cp_ = **A** _cp_ + **I** , where **I** is the Identity matrix. If we express the connection value as _−_[1] _−_[1] 1, just the more connected nodes becames the scale value larger than others. Therefore, **D**[˜] _cp_ 2 **[A]**[˜] _cp_ **[D]**[˜] _cp_ 2 is applied using the order matrix **D**[˜] _cp_ of **A**[˜] _cp_ as a method of normalizing the relationship matrix in Eqn. 3.
comments.explanation: The paper references Eqn. 3 for the GTR mathematical formulation but this equation is not provided in the context. The description mentions the GCN normalization but doesn't show the complete mathematical expression for how the convolution is performed at each node. This makes it difficult to verify the claimed relationship to RNN and understand the exact computation.

comments.title: Unverified special case claim
comments.quote: If there is no sibling node of the current node, GTR is mathematically identical to the RNN as **IW** [ _[−→] x ,[−→] h_ ]. RNN can be a special case of GTR, and the depth of the tree means times of the RNN.
comments.explanation: The claim that GTR reduces to RNN when there's no sibling node needs mathematical justification. Without seeing Eqn. 3, it's difficult to verify this claim. The expression **IW** [ _[−→] x ,[−→] h_ ] appears to have notation issues (missing brackets around the concatenated vector), and the relationship to standard RNN formulations isn't clear from the given information.

comments.title: Ambiguous attention mechanism description
comments.quote: With the attention methodology introduced in GATs(Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017), it learns how the r-th node is of importance to the q-th node. This information is replaced with the part to which the adjacency matrix is connected.
comments.explanation: The description of how attention is integrated into the GT framework is vague. It mentions replacing adjacency matrix values with attention scores but doesn't clearly specify the mathematical formulation. Equation (7) is referenced but not shown, making it difficult to understand how attention is computed and applied in the GT context.

comments.title: Inconsistent normalization notation
comments.explanation: The normalization expression appears to have notation issues. Standard GCN normalization is written as D̂^(-1/2) Â D̂^(-1/2), but here the exponents appear as subscript 2 rather than superscript -1/2. While the intent may be clear to experts, the notation is inconsistent with standard mathematical conventions and could confuse readers.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a novel structural paradigm for neural networks, 'Graph Tree Neural Networks' (GTNNs), which attempts to unify hierarchical and relational data processing. While the proposed conceptual framework is ambitious and demonstrates promise in integrating heterogeneous data types, the paper suffers from inconsistent notation, poorly defined mathematical operations in the algorithms, and informal terminology that complicates the reproducibility of the methodology. Several matrix dimensions and algorithmic steps are ambiguous or appear mathematically underspecified, particularly in the handling of the 'concatenation' and 'convolution' operations within the GTNN stack.

comments.title: Ambiguous Mathematical Notation in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 15: [ \vec{h} \mathbf{x} \mathbf{[′]} pp, i, \mathbf{h} 2 \mathbf{\leftarrow[′]} p ] \gets \mathbf{GTNN} Stack(( \mathbf{ChildrenList} \mathbf{A} _c, \mathbf{x} _\mathbf{′}p[,], \mathbf{[ h]} \mathbf{[′][p][, lv]} )[)]
comments.explanation: The algorithmic notation in Algorithm 1, line 15, is highly garbled. It is unclear what the 'GTNN Stack' function returns, what its arguments represent (due to the brackets and separators being inconsistent), or how it maps to an actual neural network layer. The use of concatenated subscripts like 'h[′][p][, lv]' and extraneous square brackets makes this step impossible to interpret mathematically.

comments.title: Inconsistent Dimension Definitions for Weights
comments.explanation: The notation for the weight matrix $W$ in Section 2.4.1 is confusing. It appears to list dimensions as sets of brackets, such as '[F][ ′][\times][(] [F][ +] [B][+][F][ ′][)]'. This is not standard mathematical notation and provides no clear understanding of the matrix shape ($F' \times (F+B+F')$ is a likely guess, but the extra brackets and '+' sign are non-standard and ambiguous).

comments.title: Underspecified Convolution Logic in Eqn 3
comments.quote: We applied the GCN methodology(Kipf & Welling, 2016), which is useful in the GNN field. Therefore we expressed as \tilde{\mathbf{A}} _cp = \mathbf{A} _cp + \mathbf{I} ... \tilde{\mathbf{D}} _cp 2 \mathbf{[A]}\tilde{\mathbf{A}} _cp \mathbf{[D]}\tilde{\mathbf{D}} _cp 2 is applied
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the GCN normalization $\tilde{D}^{-1/2} \tilde{A} \tilde{D}^{-1/2}$, but the text writes it as '$\tilde{D}_{cp} 2 [A]\tilde{A}_{cp} [D]\tilde{D}_{cp} 2$'. The use of '2' as a superscript/index suffix and the arbitrary placement of brackets make the expression unreadable. Furthermore, the relationship between this matrix and the actual feature transformation is missing, leaving the convolution operation itself mathematically undefined.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Concatenation' for Multi-head Attention
comments.quote: The results from multiple heads are concatenated and delivered to a parent node.
comments.explanation: While 'concatenated' is common in Deep Learning, the paper fails to specify the axis of concatenation or whether a final linear projection is applied to the concatenated vector. This is critical for maintaining consistency in hidden state dimensions, as suggested by later references to $F'_{lv}=128$. Without this specification, the architectural flow from the hidden state of children to the parent node is incomplete.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel data structure (Graph Tree) and two neural network models (GTC and GTR) that aim to handle multiple data types and structures in a unified manner. The experiments demonstrate potential, but the paper suffers from several significant shortcomings: key equations are missing or garbled (likely due to OCR errors), the methodology for constructing multi-type graph trees in Experiment 2 is critically underspecified, and some claims (e.g., mathematical equivalence to RNN/MLP) are made without sufficient caveats. The baseline performance for Speech Commands (100% accuracy) is suspicious and may indicate a data or methodological issue. These problems substantially undermine reproducibility and the clarity of the proposed contributions.

comments.title: Missing or garbled key equations
comments.quote:
The Ψ extracts the feature vector( −→ x p ) from xp of GTNp through the feature extraction process(Eqn.2) considering the type information(τp).
we expressed as A˜cp = Acp + I, where I is the Identity matrix.
−→ In Eqn.7, ⊙ indicates point-wise and x−→′ p is a stack of −→ x′ pq as ∥N q=0 p x′ T pq and it is x′ p∈R Np×(F+B).
comments.explanation: The paper references equations (Eqn.2, Eqn.3, Eqn.7) that are not fully displayed in the provided text. These equations are central to understanding the feature extraction, graph convolution, and attention mechanisms. Without them, the mathematical foundation of the models is incomplete. This appears to be an OCR artifact, but as presented, the paper lacks necessary formal definitions.

comments.title: Underspecified graph tree construction for multi-type datasets
comments.quote: The second experiment is about whether the output( −→ h root ) of GTNN is embedded information on all data contained in the GT(association). Therefore, we performed image, sound, and text classification by putting image, sound, text data in one GT, and we also learned the GT used in the first experiment simultaneously. One problem is that when the three types of data are combined, The number of combinations of the dataset becomes too huge. ... For this problem, we used a sampling method. When loading each data, two data of different types are sampled randomly. Thus, when we load one data, two GTs are generated of the one type GT and the three type GT.
comments.explanation: The method for constructing the GT that contains multiple data types is described only at a high level. Crucial details are missing: how are the different nodes (e.g., image, sound, text) arranged in the tree? What is the structure of the tree—does it have a root that fuses inputs from three child subtrees, or is it a flat structure? How are sibling relationships defined? The sampling strategy is also vague: what does 'two data of different types are sampled randomly' mean exactly—are pairs formed, and how are the one-type and three-type GTs generated per batch? Without this specification, the experiment cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Suspiciously high baseline for Speech Commands
comments.quote: In Table 1: for Transfer learning, SC (Speech Commands) FE Baseline accuracy is 100.0%; in Table 2, for fine-tuning, SC baseline is also 100.0%.
comments.explanation: Achieving 100% accuracy on the Speech Commands dataset (which typically has ~35 classes and is known to be challenging) is highly unusual and suggests possible data leakage, overfitting, or an error in evaluation (e.g., using the validation set incorrectly). This casts doubt on the reliability of all baseline numbers and, consequently, on the comparative claims made about GTNN performance relative to baselines.

comments.title: Ambiguous equivalence claims for GTR/GTC
comments.quote:
If there is no sibling node of the current node, GTR is mathematically identical to the RNN as IW[−→x,−→h].
If the input size of Flv is 0 and there is no sibling node of the current node, this network is mathematically identical to the FC layer as IWlv−→x T and the MLP as (IW0 .. (IWlv−1(IWlv−→x T)))
comments.explanation: The notation 'IW' is unclear (likely a formatting error; possibly intended as a weight matrix W). More importantly, the conditions 'no sibling node' and 'input size 0' are highly restrictive. For GTR to be identical to an RNN, the tree must be a linear chain, and the node types must align with time steps. For GTC to be identical to an FC/MLP, the tree must again be a chain and nodes must have no input features (input size 0) — which contradicts the notion of the node containing data. These special cases are possible but the phrasing 'mathematically identical' without emphasizing these strong constraints is misleading. A reader could infer a more general equivalence than holds.

comments.title: Insufficient detail on feature extraction network integration
comments.quote: We used several benchmark datasets (image(MNIST(LeCun et al., 1998)), sound(Speech Commands(Warden, 2018)), text(IMDB(Maas et al., 2011),SST(Socher et al., 2013)), graph(Dou et al., 2021)).
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify how the feature extraction networks (LeNet-5, M5, CNN) are connected to the GTNN. For instance, are the output features from these networks directly fed into the GT nodes as x vectors? Are the networks trained end-to-end with the GTNN, or are they fixed? The training details (learning rate, optimizer, batch size, etc.) for the joint models are not provided. While some details may be in the appendix (not available), the main text lacks sufficient information for reproducibility of the key experiments.

comments.title: Overstatement in claims about data-driven depth adjustment
comments.quote: GTNNs are data-driven learning in which the number of convolutions varies according to the depth of the tree. Instead of using fixed sequence layers, we create a graph tree for each data and learn according to the tree’s structure.
comments.explanation: This claim gives the impression that the network automatically adjusts its depth based on data complexity. In reality, the depth is predetermined by the tree structure assigned to each data point (as shown in Figure 4, the trees have fixed depths like 3 or 2). While the tree depth can vary across different data points if designed that way, the paper does not propose or evaluate any method for automatically determining the appropriate depth. The claim is technically true in the sense that the GT defines the number of convolutions, but it is not a novel adaptive mechanism as the phrasing suggests.

comments.title: Confusing variable naming in Algorithm 1 (DFC)
comments.quote: GTNp.more ← (∗i1, ∗i2) from lines 11 and 16.
comments.explanation: The variables ∗i1 and ∗i2 are used to store information from the feature extraction (line 3) and from the GTNN stack (line 14), but these names are not intuitive. While the text later explains that they are used for deconvolution (e.g., maxpool indices), the use of asterisks and generic names makes the algorithm harder to follow. This is a minor clarity issue.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel Graph Tree Neural Network (GTNN) architecture that combines hierarchical tree structures with sibling relationships, leveraging depth-first traversal for forward (DFC) and backward (DFD) propagation. The core idea of dynamically adapting network depth and width to dataset-specific graph trees is conceptually interesting and aligns well with modern data-driven architecture paradigms. However, the manuscript suffers from several technical ambiguities, particularly around dimensionality assumptions, algorithmic pseudocode that mixes programming syntax with mathematical operations, and attention normalization practices that deviate from standard formulations without justification. While the experimental results demonstrate promising multi-modal capability, the claims of 'no significant degradation' in simultaneous learning are somewhat overstated when compared to the baseline tables. Careful revision of the mathematical definitions, clarification of the aggregation and attention mechanisms, and more precise reporting of performance deltas would significantly strengthen the paper's reproducibility and scholarly rigor.

comments.title: Unexplained dimensionality in GTC weight matrix
comments.quote: There is **W** _lv ∈ R _[F] lv[ ′][×]_( _[F][lv]_[+] _[B]_[+] _[F] lv[ ′]_ +1[)]_ in charge of each level, and the network in charge of each level is called the level layer.
comments.explanation: The author defines the weight matrix for GTC with an unexplained `+1` in the input dimension: `F_lv + B + F'_lv + 1`. The preceding text describes the input to a node as a concatenation of the node's own feature vector `x` (size `F+B`) and the stacked hidden states of its children (size `F'_lv` per child, aggregated across `N_p` siblings). There is no mention of an additional scalar input, positional encoding, or bias term being concatenated to the feature vector before multiplication. This extra dimension lacks justification and contradicts the dimensional analysis of the data flow described earlier. It likely stems from an undocumented feature, a bias-vector concatenation convention, or a typo. Without clarification, reproducing the exact architecture or verifying gradient shapes is impossible.

comments.title: Ambiguous `Stack()` operation in DFC algorithm
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 relies on a `Stack()` operation to combine the adjacency matrix `A_c`, the node's extracted feature `x'_p`, the aggregated child hidden states `h'_p`, and the level index `lv`. However, the exact mathematical composition of this stack is left implicit. Specifically, it is unclear how the `N×N` adjacency matrix `A_c` interacts with the `N×(F+B+F')` stacked feature/hidden vectors to form the input for the GCN/linear layer. Standard graph convolutions require `[A] * [X]` or row-wise permutations, but the algorithm's `Stack()` does not specify whether this multiplication occurs inside or outside the stack, how dimensions align for the forward pass, or how gradients flow through this operation. This vagueness makes it difficult to implement the exact propagation step or verify the intended encoding of sibling relationships.

comments.title: Attention normalization deviates from standard practice without justification
comments.quote:
If **A** _cp_ does not exist in the GTN, we utilized to select critical features as : 


The self-node is set to 1 and the relationship values of the node are replaced with a real value of 0 _∼_ 1 using Sigmoid. Therefore, we can train the attention learning without **A** _cp_ .
comments.explanation: The authors describe adapting GAT-style attention for nodes without sibling connections. Standard GAT computes attention scores via learned projections, followed by a softmax over neighbors to ensure weights sum to 1. The text states that without `A_cp`, attention values are replaced with a sigmoid (yielding 0~1) and a self-node is set to 1. However, it is unclear how these raw attention weights are normalized for the weighted aggregation step. If softmax is skipped in favor of sigmoid, the scale of the aggregated vector will depend on the raw output magnitudes and number of heads rather than being properly normalized, which can destabilize training and contradict the GAT formulation referenced in Sec 2.5.1. The missing equation (likely an OCR artifact) compounds this, but the textual description itself lacks the mathematical rigor needed to reproduce the attention mechanism correctly.

comments.title: Overstated claim on simultaneous learning performance
comments.quote: As a result, these models learned without significant performance degradation, and the output vector contained all the information in the GT.
comments.explanation: The abstract and conclusion assert that simultaneous multi-modal learning causes 'no significant performance degradation.' However, Table 1 shows that for the 'Task Layers / 47 class' setup (raw simultaneous learning), GTC on IMDB drops from 87.13% (FE Baseline) to 85.38% (~1.75% absolute drop), and GTR drops from 86.10% to 85.62%. The authors themselves acknowledge later that 'M5 needs to be more trained, and CNN has an overfitting problem' in simultaneous mode, and only recover near-baseline performance using transfer learning and fine-tuning. While ~1.8% is not catastrophic, it is statistically and practically noticeable, especially on a relatively small dataset like IMDB. The claim of 'no significant degradation' is therefore an overstatement of the raw simultaneous learning results, though it accurately describes the adapted (transfer/fine-tuned) results.

comments.title: Undefined argument `I` in propagation algorithm
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 3 (`PROPAGATE`), the argument `I` is passed to both `GTNN` and `GTNN^{-1}`. Earlier in Section 2.4.1, `I` was explicitly defined as an identity matrix used for GCN normalization (`~A_cp = A_cp + I`). However, in the algorithm, `I` appears alongside feature vectors and hyperparameters like `0` (likely representing level or batch index). It is unclear whether `I` here refers to the identity matrix (which would be dimensionally incompatible with a feature vector input), a specific input data placeholder, a learnable initial state, or a typo for a different symbol. This ambiguity obscures the initialization or input requirements for the encoding/decoding step and could mislead readers attempting to implement the autoencoder-style propagation.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes Graph Tree Neural Networks (GTNN) as a data-driven alternative to fixed-layer architectures for processing hierarchical and multi-modal data. The authors provide a compelling motivation by comparing the GT architecture to human sensory integration and attempt to unify learning across images, sounds, and text. While the experimental results demonstrate competitive performance, the mathematical descriptions of the proposed mechanisms, particularly the feature extraction process and the data sampling strategy, contain conceptual ambiguities that could mislead a reader on the model's actual behavior. Specifically, the mechanism for handling biases differs from standard notation, and the sampling method for multi-modal experiments is underspecified, making it difficult to reproduce the findings with confidence.
